message.content: Adversaries may exploit Boot or Logon Initialization Scripts to establish persistence on a system by leveraging scripts that automatically execute during boot or logon. These scripts, which can perform administrative tasks and execute other programs, vary by operating system and can be applied locally or remotely. The exploitation of these scripts allows adversaries to maintain persistence and potentially escalate privileges, as some scripts run with higher privileges. Common methods include modifying existing scripts or adding new ones, as seen with Rocke's use of an "init.d" startup script. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting changes to initialization scripts, employing strict access controls, and regularly auditing scripts for unauthorized modifications.
